skill.name: component-refactoring
skill.description: Guides safe refactoring of high-complexity Dify React components by using component splitting, hook extraction, complexity analysis, and incremental verification workflows.
skill.summary: Refactor large Dify React components with focused hooks, smaller components, and complexity checks.
skill.author: langgenius

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples, file names, or ordinary TypeScript identifiers. The only confirmed risk factor is legitimate external command guidance for pnpm scripts, which should be used only in trusted Dify repositories. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, destructive command, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: External Command Guidance Requires Trusted Repository Context
low_findings.description: The skill tells users to run pnpm refactoring, analysis, lint, type-check, and test commands from the Dify web directory. These commands are normal for a coding skill, but they execute local package scripts and may modify files through lint fixes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command instructions are explicit and repeated, but they are documented workflow commands rather than hidden execution logic. The risk is low when used in a trusted repository with reviewed package scripts.

low_findings.title: Static High-Severity Patterns Dismissed as False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and sensitive file detections map to normal words such as key, mode, publish, and Object.keys in examples. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, reconnaissance commands, credential files, or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of representative flagged lines shows ordinary TypeScript examples and prose, not security-sensitive behavior. Searches did not find prompt injection, network exfiltration, environment access, or destructive shell commands.

content.value_statement: Large React components slow reviews and make tests harder to trust. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused workflow for safer Dify frontend refactoring.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when Dify React components should be refactored before testing.
Guides extraction of custom hooks for state, effects, API calls, and form logic.
Explains component splitting patterns for large UI sections, modals, and repeated items.
Uses Dify-specific directory, naming, service hook, and workflow node conventions.
Recommends complexity checks with pnpm analyze-component and refactor-component commands.
Promotes incremental lint, type-check, test, and manual verification after each change.
limitations: It is tailored to the Dify frontend structure and may not fit unrelated React projects.
It does not execute refactoring automatically without an agent or user applying changes.
It depends on local pnpm scripts and repository tooling being available.
It focuses on structure and complexity, not visual redesign or product behavior changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Large Components for Tests
use_cases.description: Break a complex Dify component into smaller units before adding reliable tests.

use_cases.title: Standardize Reviewable Refactors
use_cases.description: Create an incremental plan that keeps naming, exports, and service hooks aligned with Dify conventions.

use_cases.title: Extract Reusable React Hooks
use_cases.description: Move coupled state, effects, validation, or data fetching into focused hooks with clear return values.

prompt_templates.title: Find Refactoring Targets
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: identify whether a component needs refactoring.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Dify React component and decide if it should be refactored before testing. Use the component-refactoring skill and explain the main reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Component Split
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: split a large component into focused files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the component-refactoring skill to create a step-by-step split plan for this component. Preserve Dify naming, exports, and directory patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Hook Logic
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: move state and effects into a custom hook.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the component-refactoring skill to extract related state, effects, and handlers into a custom hook. Keep behavior unchanged and list verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Reduce Complexity Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: refactor a high-complexity component with metrics.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the component-refactoring workflow for this high-complexity Dify component. Use analyzer results, propose incremental changes, and verify each step.

output_examples.input: A 500-line configuration page mixes header UI, model settings, modal state, and publishing actions.
output: A section-based split plan for header, configuration body, operations, and modal management.
A suggested hook for grouped model configuration state and update handlers.
A verification checklist covering lint, type-check, tests, and manual behavior checks.

output_examples.input: A workflow node component has complex conditionals, panel rendering, and event handlers in one file.
output: A proposed workflow node file structure using node, panel, interactions, and type files.
Recommendations for lookup tables or early returns to reduce conditional complexity.
Notes on preserving existing workflow node conventions and exports.

output_examples.input: A component has several related effects that update model configuration and completion parameters.
output: A custom hook extraction plan with input parameters, return values, and naming guidance.
Testing suggestions for the extracted hook and the simplified component.
Warnings about avoiding tiny hooks with no clear cohesion.

best_practices: Run complexity analysis before and after refactoring so progress is measurable.
Extract one cohesive unit at a time and verify behavior before continuing.
Keep new files near the original domain and follow existing Dify naming patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not split code into many tiny hooks that only wrap one state value.
Do not change product behavior while reducing component complexity.
Do not ignore existing directory, export, service hook, or workflow node conventions.

faq.question: What projects is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for the Dify frontend and React components that follow Dify conventions.

faq.question: Does it write code automatically?
faq.answer: It provides structured guidance for an AI assistant or user to apply code changes.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a component has high complexity, more than 300 lines, or mixed UI and business logic.

faq.question: Can it help with custom hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives patterns for extracting state, effects, form logic, API calls, and reusable behavior.

faq.question: Does it replace testing?
faq.answer: No. It recommends linting, type-checking, tests, and manual checks after each extraction.

faq.question: What security risk did the audit find?
faq.answer: The audit found only documented local pnpm command guidance, which is low risk in trusted repositories.
